title: Unions and tagged values

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Unions and tagged values; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ISO C Working Group C23 status; ISO/IEC 9899:2024; provisions applicable to Unions and tagged values
sources.claim: A union provides overlapping storage for alternative members; code must track which representation is active or otherwise permitted before reading a member.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A union provides overlapping storage for alternative members; code must track which representation is active or otherwise permitted before reading a member.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A tagged value stores an enum kind beside a union and reads integer or floating member only when the tag names that member. Your goal is to implement and safely inspect a simple tagged union. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: One-dimensional arrays; Addresses and dereference; Structures; Enumerations. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A union provides overlapping storage for alternative members; code must track which representation is active or otherwise permitted before reading a member. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Reading a non-current member has limited implementation-defined or specified cases, padding and trap representations matter, and a union is not a self-tagging variant. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: A union remembers which member was last written and prevents code from reading the wrong alternative.

includes: Understand shared storage and pair a union with a valid discriminant.
Implement and safely inspect a simple tagged union.
excludes: Reading a non-current member has limited implementation-defined or specified cases, padding and trap representations matter, and a union is not a self-tagging variant.

outcomes: Implement and safely inspect a simple tagged union.

examples.title: Unions and tagged values: defined case
examples.situation: A tagged value stores an enum kind beside a union and reads integer or floating member only when the tag names that member.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A tagged value stores an enum kind beside a union and reads integer or floating member only when the tag names that member.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: A C union is not self-tagging; maintain and validate a separate discriminator before interpreting the overlapping storage.
examples.result: The defined result follows because A C union is not self-tagging; maintain and validate a separate discriminator before interpreting the overlapping storage.

examples.title: Unions and tagged values: boundary case
examples.situation: Writing the double member and then assuming the int member contains a portable bit conversion is rejected.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Writing the double member and then assuming the int member contains a portable bit conversion is rejected.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Reading a non-current member has limited implementation-defined or specified cases, padding and trap representations matter, and a union is not a self-tagging variant.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: A union remembers which member was last written and prevents code from reading the wrong alternative.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and safely inspect a simple tagged union.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A union remembers which member was last written and prevents code from reading the wrong alternative.
misconceptions.correction: A C union is not self-tagging; maintain and validate a separate discriminator before interpreting the overlapping storage.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: A union remembers which member was last written and prevents code from reading the wrong alternative.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Unions and tagged values: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Reading a non-current member has limited implementation-defined or specified cases, padding and trap representations matter, and a union is not a self-tagging variant.

recap.summary: You should now be able to implement and safely inspect a simple tagged union. Keep this boundary in view: Reading a non-current member has limited implementation-defined or specified cases, padding and trap representations matter, and a union is not a self-tagging variant.
